title: Introducing Project Cosmos: Carbon Brief’s ‘universe’ of climate science
gist: Carbon Brief unveils Project Cosmos, an 18-month effort to build the world's largest climate-research database — 1.8m publications linked by 40m citations, anchored on the full IPCC corpus plus 22 climate journals — deliberately kept closed-source to prevent AI scraping.

insights.claim: Carbon Brief's Cosmos database contains 1,816,639 unique climate-change publications linked by just over 40m citation relationships, built over 18 months and anchored on the full IPCC corpus.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Establishes a unique proprietary climate-science asset; scale and citation graph make it a defensible data moat for bibliometric analysis.
insights.quote: The Cosmos database – which features more than 1.8m individual publications linked by 40m citation relationships

insights.claim: The number of references in IPCC reports has grown almost 40-fold over three decades, from 2,315 in the first assessment cycle to 82,523 in the sixth.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Quantifies the explosive growth of the climate-science evidence base — a proxy for the maturing scale of the field.
insights.quote: The number of references in IPCC reports has increased almost 40-fold over the past three decades.

insights.claim: More than 97% of publications in the Cosmos database are in English, with French and Spanish the most notable other languages.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Documents an English-language dominance / language bias in the foundational climate-science corpus.
insights.quote: More than 97% of these publications are in English.

insights.claim: Carbon Brief selected OpenAlex over Scopus, Web of Science and Google Scholar to source publication metadata because it is open source and one of the most complete catalogues, and used Neo4j to manage the highly connected citation data.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Validation of open-source academic infrastructure (OpenAlex, Neo4j) over incumbent paid databases for large-scale citation analysis.
insights.quote: Carbon Brief selected OpenAlex because it is open source and one of the most complete sources of data.
insights.locator: How did Carbon Brief create the database?

insights.claim: Carbon Brief deliberately omitted IPCC reports and chapters from citation-score calculations because their counts would dwarf all other publications and distort the rankings.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Methodological choice that shapes the entire Cosmos 500 ranking — relevant for interpreting downstream author/institution rankings.
insights.locator: How has Carbon Brief analysed the data?

insights.claim: Carbon Brief chose deliberately not to make the Cosmos database open source due to the risk of its contents being scraped and used by AI bots.
insights.speaker: Leo Hickman, Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
insights.so_what: Notable signal of publishers withholding curated data from AI training — part of the broader content-vs-AI access tension.
insights.quote: Carbon Brief has chosen deliberately not to make the database open source, due to the risk of its contents being scraped and used by AI bots.
insights.locator: How can researchers access the database?

source_meta.author: Leo Hickman, Ayesha Tandon, Kerry Cleaver, Joe Goodman, Tom Pearson, Tom Prater
